subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
- **51 galaxy tiles** (hexagonal system tiles forming the modular game board)
- **17+ faction sheets** with unique abilities, starting units, technologies, and flagships
- **354 plastic unit miniatures** (Fighters, Infantry, Carriers, Destroyers, Cruisers, Dreadnoughts, War Suns, PDS, Space Docks, Flagships, Mechs)
- **8 Strategy Cards** (numbered 1-8, each with a unique ability)
- **Public Objective cards** (Stage I and Stage II)
- **Secret Objective cards** (personal hidden goals)
- **Action Cards** (tactical advantages, sabotages, political maneuvering)
- **Agenda Cards** (galactic laws voted on during the Agenda Phase)
- **Technology cards** (4 color-coded tech trees: Blue/Propulsion, Red/Warfare, Yellow/Cybernetic, Green/Biotic)
- **Planet cards** (resource and influence values for each planet)
- **Trade Good tokens**
- **Command tokens** (placed in Tactics, Fleet, and Strategy pools)
- **Control tokens** and **attachment tokens**
- **Victory Point track** (first to 10 VP wins)
- **Speaker token** (determines first pick of Strategy Cards)

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
1. Place **Mecatol Rex** (the galactic capital, worth VP) in the center of the table.
2. **Build the galaxy**: Players take turns placing system tiles around Mecatol Rex following setup rules. Tiles contain planets with resource and influence values, anomalies (asteroids, nebulae, supernovae, gravity rifts), and empty space. Each player's **home system** is placed at the galaxy edge.
3. Shuffle and place **Stage I Public Objectives** face-down; reveal 2. These are shared goals all players compete to score.
4. Shuffle **Stage II Public Objectives** separately.
5. Shuffle the **Agenda deck** and place it near the board.
6. Shuffle **Action Cards**, **Secret Objectives**, and place the **8 Strategy Cards** in view.
7. Set the **VP track** — game ends when someone reaches **10 VP**.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
1. Each player selects (or is dealt) a **faction**. Each faction has:
   - Unique faction abilities
   - A unique Flagship unit
   - Faction-specific technologies
   - Starting units, planets, and technologies
   - A promissory note (tradeable political promise)
2. Place your **home system tile** and starting units as shown on your faction sheet.
3. Take your starting **technology cards** and **planet cards**.
4. Receive **Command Tokens** — distribute them among your 3 pools:
   - **Tactics Pool**: Used to activate systems (move/attack). Each activation costs 1 token.
   - **Fleet Pool**: Determines max non-fighter ships in each system.
   - **Strategy Pool**: Used to execute secondary abilities of Strategy Cards.
5. Draw **2 Secret Objectives**, keep 1 (hidden until scored).
6. The **Speaker** (first player) is determined by setup rules or faction abilities.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content:
Twilight Imperium (4th Edition) is the definitive space opera board game. Players control unique galactic civilizations vying for dominance in the wake of a fallen empire. The game combines military conquest, political negotiation, economic management, and technological development.

The game is played over multiple rounds, each consisting of a **Strategy Phase**, **Action Phase**, and **Status Phase** (with an **Agenda Phase** once Mecatol Rex is claimed). Victory is achieved by scoring **10 Victory Points** through public and secret objectives — NOT by military conquest alone. You can conquer the entire galaxy and still lose if you haven't scored objectives.

The political Agenda Phase, unique faction abilities, and negotiation-heavy gameplay make this far more than a war game — it's a full simulation of galactic civilization.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
## Round Structure

### 1. Strategy Phase
Starting with the **Speaker** and going clockwise, each player selects **one Strategy Card** (numbered 1-8). Each card grants a unique powerful ability and determines turn order for the round (lower number = earlier turns). Strategy Cards:

| # | Name | Primary Ability |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Leadership | Gain Command Tokens |
| 2 | Diplomacy | Protect a system from attack; ready exhausted planets |
| 3 | Politics | Draw Action Cards; become the new Speaker; look at Agenda cards |
| 4 | Construction | Build structures (PDS, Space Docks) without activating |
| 5 | Trade | Replenish commodities; establish trade agreements |
| 6 | Warfare | Move after building; remove a command token from the board |
| 7 | Technology | Research a technology |
| 8 | Imperial | Score a public objective; draw Secret Objectives; gain VP if you control Mecatol Rex |

Each card also has a **Secondary Ability** that all OTHER players may use (by spending a Command Token from their Strategy pool).

### 2. Action Phase
Players take turns in **Strategy Card number order** (lowest goes first). On your turn, perform ONE of the following:

#### a) Tactical Action (Most Common)
1. **Activate a system** by placing a Command Token from your Tactics pool on it.
2. **Move ships** from adjacent systems into the activated system.
3. **Space Combat** — if enemy ships are present, resolve combat.
4. **Invasion** — land Ground Forces on planets to take control.
5. **Production** — build new units at Space Docks in the activated system.

#### b) Strategic Action
Use your chosen Strategy Card's **Primary Ability**. Then, in turn order, all other players may use the **Secondary Ability** (by spending a Strategy pool token).

#### c) Component Action
Use an ability on a card that says "Action:" (some technologies, action cards, faction abilities).

#### d) Pass
If you have no more actions to take (or choose not to), pass. You cannot take more actions this round once you pass.

The Action Phase ends when all players have passed.

### 3. Status Phase
1. **Score Objectives**: Each player may score 1 Public Objective and 1 Secret Objective (if they meet the requirements).
2. **Reveal a new Public Objective** (Stage I in early rounds, Stage II in later rounds).
3. **Draw Action Cards** (up to hand limit).
4. **Remove Command Tokens** from the board (resetting activated systems).
5. **Gain and redistribute Command Tokens** (2 new tokens + redistribute existing).
6. **Ready exhausted cards** (planet cards, technology cards, etc.).
7. **Repair damaged units**.
8. **Return Strategy Cards** to the common pool.

### 4. Agenda Phase (After Mecatol Rex Is Claimed)
Once any player controls Mecatol Rex, an **Agenda Phase** occurs at the end of every subsequent round:
1. The Speaker reveals the **top Agenda Card** — a galactic law or directive.
2. Players discuss, negotiate, threaten, and bribe each other.
3. Players vote using **Influence** from their exhausted planet cards. Each influence point = 1 vote.
4. The outcome with the most votes takes effect (can be a permanent law or one-time resolution).
5. Repeat for a **second Agenda Card**.

Agendas can dramatically change the game — electing players to powerful positions, granting or removing VP, restricting actions, or changing rules.

subtopics.title: Key Actions
subtopics.content:
### Combat
Space combat occurs when ships from different players occupy the same system:
1. **Anti-Fighter Barrage**: Destroyers fire first at enemy fighters.
2. **Space Combat Rounds**: Each ship rolls 1 die (some roll more). A roll meeting or exceeding the unit's **combat value** scores a hit. Opponent assigns hits to their ships.
3. **Sustain Damage**: Dreadnoughts, War Suns, Flagships, and Mechs can absorb 1 hit (become damaged instead of destroyed).
4. Repeat rounds until one side is destroyed or retreats.

**Ground Combat** (invasion) works similarly but with Infantry and Mechs fighting for planet control. PDS (Planetary Defense Systems) can fire into space or defend against invaders.

### Technology
Research technologies by spending resources and meeting **prerequisites** (must own prerequisite-colored technologies):
- **Blue (Propulsion)**: Movement and fleet upgrades (Gravity Drive, Fleet Logistics).
- **Red (Warfare)**: Combat upgrades (Assault Cannon, Duranium Armor).
- **Yellow (Cybernetic)**: Economic and production upgrades (Sarween Tools, Transit Diodes).
- **Green (Biotic)**: Infantry and planetary upgrades (Hyper Metabolism, X-89 Bacterial Weapon).
- **Unit Upgrades**: Improved versions of your ships (Cruiser II, Dreadnought II, etc.).

### Trade and Negotiation
Players can negotiate **anything** at any time:
- Trade goods, promissory notes, and commodities.
- Binding and non-binding deals (only exchanges of game components are enforceable — verbal promises are not).
- Ceasefire agreements, support for objectives, vote-trading for Agenda Phase.
- Promissory notes are faction-specific tradeable promises (e.g., "I will not attack your home system").

### Objectives — The Path to Victory
You win by scoring **10 VP**, primarily through objectives:
- **Stage I Public Objectives** (1 VP each): e.g., "Spend 8 resources," "Control 4 planets in non-home systems."
- **Stage II Public Objectives** (2 VP each): e.g., "Spend 16 resources," "Win a space combat against a player who has the most VP."
- **Secret Objectives** (1 VP each): e.g., "Destroy another player's flagship," "Control Mecatol Rex and 3 planets in adjacent systems."
- **Imperial Strategy Card**: Grants VP for controlling Mecatol Rex.
- **Agenda Phase**: Some agendas award VP.
- **Custodians Token**: First player to claim Mecatol Rex removes the Custodians Token for 1 VP.

### Mecatol Rex
The galactic capital at the center of the map:
- Controlling it triggers the Agenda Phase.
- The Imperial Strategy Card rewards VP for holding it.
- It's a high-value target that everyone wants — expect conflict here.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
### Faction Asymmetry
Each of the 17+ factions plays dramatically differently:
- **Federation of Sol**: Versatile humans with strong infantry and command token economy.
- **Barony of Letnev**: Military powerhouse with the ability to spend trade goods for combat rerolls.
- **Emirates of Hacan**: Master traders who can trade action cards and facilitate deals.
- **Universities of Jol-Nar**: Tech prodigies who start with extra technologies but fight poorly.
- **Xxcha Kingdom**: Defensive turtle faction with political power and PDS upgrades.
- **Yssaril Tribes**: Action card manipulators with extra draws and skip abilities.
- **Clan of Saar**: Nomadic faction with mobile Space Docks.
- **L1Z1X Mindnet**: Aggressive assimilators with powerful Dreadnoughts.
- And many more, each with unique mechanics that fundamentally change strategy.

### Command Tokens — Action Economy
Command Tokens are the game's most precious resource:
- **Tactics Pool**: Each activation costs 1 token. More tokens = more actions per round.
- **Fleet Pool**: Determines max non-fighter ships in each system. More tokens = bigger fleets.
- **Strategy Pool**: Spent to use Secondary Abilities of others' Strategy Cards.

Balancing token allocation across these three pools is a critical ongoing decision.

### Promissory Notes
Each faction has a unique promissory note that can be traded to other players:
- When the holder plays it, the effect occurs (often beneficial to the holder, costly to the original owner).
- Examples: "Ceasefire" (can't attack the original owner next round), "Trade Convoys" (share commodities).

### Action Cards
Draw from the Action Card deck during the Status Phase. Cards provide tactical advantages:
- **Sabotage**: Cancel another player's Action Card.
- **Direct Hit**: Destroy a damaged ship (bypasses Sustain Damage).
- **Skilled Retreat**: Retreat from combat before it resolves.
- **Political shenanigans**: Manipulate voting, steal objectives, etc.

### Wormholes
Some systems contain wormholes (Alpha and Beta). All systems with the same wormhole type are considered **adjacent** for movement purposes, enabling rapid long-distance travel.

### Anomalies
- **Asteroid Fields**: Ships cannot move through (except with certain technologies).
- **Nebulae**: Ships moving through are slowed; defenders in nebulae gain combat bonuses.
- **Supernovae**: Impassable and unclaimable.
- **Gravity Rifts**: Moving through requires a roll — risk losing ships, but gain extra movement.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
The game ends **immediately** when a player reaches **10 Victory Points** (checked during the Status Phase). That player wins.

VP sources:
- **Stage I Public Objectives**: 1 VP each (scored during Status Phase, max 1 per round).
- **Stage II Public Objectives**: 2 VP each.
- **Secret Objectives**: 1 VP each (max 3 total across the game).
- **Imperial Strategy Card**: 1 VP for controlling Mecatol Rex when played.
- **Custodians Token**: 1 VP (first player to take Mecatol Rex).
- **Agenda Phase**: Some laws award VP.
- **Support for the Throne**: Promissory note that gives 1 VP to the holder.

**Important**: You can only score 1 Public Objective and 1 Secret Objective per Status Phase. This means the game takes a minimum of several rounds regardless of military strength — you need patience and planning.

If no player has reached 10 VP when the objective deck runs out, the player with the most VP at that point wins.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Score objectives, not kills**. Military strength is a means to an end. You win by scoring objectives, not by destroying opponents. Focus on what objectives are available and work toward them.
- **Don't turtle in your home system**. You need to expand to score objectives. Take your slice of the galaxy early.
- **Pick Strategy Cards based on what you need**, not just the number. Technology and Warfare are powerful early. Imperial is critical once Mecatol Rex is contested.
- **Negotiate constantly**. This is a negotiation game as much as a strategy game. Trade, make deals, and build (temporary) alliances.
- **Watch the VP track**. The player closest to 10 VP is the biggest threat. Coordinate with others to slow the leader.
- **Mecatol Rex matters**. Controlling it triggers the Agenda Phase and enables scoring via Imperial. Don't ignore it, but don't overcommit either.

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
- **Objective drafting**: Plan 2-3 rounds ahead. Look at revealed Public Objectives and your Secret Objectives — build your entire strategy around scoring them efficiently.
- **Technology path**: Choose a focused tech path. Going wide across all 4 colors is inefficient. Most factions benefit from rushing a specific Unit Upgrade II.
- **King-making awareness**: In TI4, attacking the leader is a communal responsibility. If you let one player run away with VP, everyone loses. Conversely, attacking the leader makes you their enemy — coordinate with allies.
- **Timing the Imperial card**: Taking the Imperial Strategy Card at the right moment (when you control Mecatol Rex and can score an objective) can swing multiple VP in one round.
- **Trade good economy**: Trade goods are the universal currency. Hoard them for critical moments — they can be spent as resources or influence.
- **Action card management**: Hold Sabotage for critical moments. Direct Hit is devastating against Dreadnoughts and War Suns. Timing is everything.
- **Political capital**: The Agenda Phase is immensely powerful. Voting for or against laws can permanently alter the game state. Build political alliances and save influence for critical votes.
- **Slice evaluation**: During galaxy setup, identify which systems are most valuable in your slice and prioritize them. Evaluate neighbor factions — are they aggressive or peaceful? Plan accordingly.
